--- SOURCE 2: The Future of AI and Informatics in Radiology: 10 Predictions ---
URL: https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114

SUMMARY:
Radiology is a ripe target for AI because imaging data have been digital for decades. This progress sets the stage for a unified radiology workstation, with image display, reporting, and AI seamlessly integrated into a cloud-based cockpit (10) that dramatically improves radiologist efficiency. An automatically protocoled imaging study will arrive in the radiologist’s work queue preprocessed by AI algorithms, with patient history summarized, organs segmented and measured, abnormalities highlighted, and a report drafted. The Medical Imaging and Data Resource Center, or MIDRC, funded by the National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering, has published over 100 000 imaging studies from sites around the United States (38), serving as a model for large, diverse public medical data sets.

_The computer as an intellectual tool can reshape the present system of health care, fundamentally alter the role of the physician, and profoundly change the nature of medical manpower recruitment and medical education._

–William B. Schwartz, 1970 ([1](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r1))

Introduction
------------

Artificial intelligence (AI) and informatics are transforming radiology. Ten years ago, no expert would have predicted today’s vibrant radiology AI industry with over 100 AI companies and nearly 400 radiology AI algorithms cleared by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). And less than a year ago, not even the savviest prognosticators would have believed that these algorithms could produce poetry, win fine art competitions, and pass the medical boards. Now, as we celebrate the centennial of our specialty’s flagship journal, _Radiology_, these accomplishments are part of our reality.

Moments of daunting transformation can liberate us to dream big. With that in mind, here are 10 predictions for the future of AI and informatics in radiology ([Table](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#tbl1)).

**Ten Predictions for the Future of AI and Informatics in Radiology**![Image 11](https://pubs.rsna.org/cms/10.1148/radiol.231114/asset/images/medium/radiol.231114.tbl1.png)

1. Radiology Will Continue to Lead the Way for AI in Medicine
-------------------------------------------------------------

In the current health care market, AI tools for radiologists dominate. Seventy-five percent of the over 500 FDA-cleared AI algorithms target radiology practice, increasing from 70% in 2021 ([2](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r2)). Radiology is a ripe target for AI because imaging data have been digital for decades. Our exabytes of digital imaging data are more objective than other clinical data because they are governed by the laws of physics rather than by subjective signs and symptoms expressed in a narrative note. And pairing images with descriptive text reports makes them ideal for the creation of accurate machine learning algorithms ([3](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r3)).

About 4% of diagnostic interpretations contain clinically significant errors ([4](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r4)). Errors arise because many image interpretation tasks are not well suited for human capabilities. For example, finding a small nodule nestled among the pulmonary vessels is akin to a “needle in a haystack.” Accurately quantifying abnormalities, such as the size of an irregularly shaped tumor or the amount of calcium in the coronary arteries, can also challenge human capabilities. Correlating complex multimodal clinical data sources, such as radiology, genomics, and pathology, may be beyond human capabilities. But AI algorithms can readily perform these tasks. Thus, AI researchers will continue to develop these new capabilities as a complement to human perception.

2. Virtual Assistants Will Draft Radiology Reports and Address Radiologist Burnout
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Teaching trainees can be one of the most rewarding aspects of academic radiology. But teaching at the workstation can slow the productivity of attending radiologists ([5](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r5)). Yet, trainees provide powerful productivity advantages: They preview the imaging study, draft a report, edit it based on feedback, and route it to the attending radiologist for final signature, enabling attending radiologists to focus on the big picture—putting the findings in appropriate context ([6](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r6)).

Virtual assistants will bring these same efficiencies to radiologists who do not have the privilege of working with trainees. The combination of computer vision algorithms, which analyze images to identify findings, and large language models (LLMs), which are trained on massive data sets to generate text, will make this possible ([Figure](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#fig1)). Some computer vision algorithms can detect more than 70 findings in a single imaging study ([7](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r7)). Prompted by this list of findings, an LLM will draft a radiology report ([8](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r8)). Finally, the radiologist will edit and sign the report. The AI models could be periodically retrained from feedback obtained by comparing draft and final reports.

The system would present the radiologist with a draft report for editing and signature.

These AI-based virtual assistants will not only relieve the drudgery of dictating long radiology reports but will also upskill advanced practice providers to address the chronic radiologist shortage. These changes will occur first for repetitive studies, such as bedside chest radiographs, and then for a wide variety of common imaging studies.

3. An Intelligent Image Interpretation Cockpit Will Become as Pervasive as Email
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When picture archiving and communication systems first became available, they required custom monitors, dedicated high-performance networks, and expensive bespoke storage devices. Early speech recognition systems required powerful desktop computers. And most medical record systems were still on paper. These disparate technologies, today the mainstay of the radiologist’s desktop, evolved separately and have never worked together well. Thus, it is not surprising that radiologists often work with disjointed system integrations and clashing user interfaces.

Recent technological progress makes a unified system possible. See, for example, the editorial on the maturing of imaging informatics by Chang in this issue of _Radiology_ ([9](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r9)). Cloud computing and storage are just as secure as hospital data centers for storing health care data. Siri, Alexa, and Google already use cloud-based speech recognition. And AI algorithms can be deployed easily in the cloud. This progress sets the stage for a unified radiology workstation, with image display, reporting, and AI seamlessly integrated into a cloud-based cockpit ([10](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r10)) that dramatically improves radiologist efficiency. An automatically protocoled imaging study will arrive in the radiologist’s work queue preprocessed by AI algorithms, with patient history summarized, organs segmented and measured, abnormalities highlighted, and a report drafted. The radiologist will modify the report by using speech recognition or by clicking the image and choosing from a structured list of suggested imaging findings. These cloud-native capabilities will make virtual collaboration with clinical colleagues, including live video, immediate and seamless.

4. Highly Sensitive AI Will Reduce the Need for Human Image Interpretation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

Until the advent of modern machine learning methods in the past few years, it was unthinkable that some radiology studies would never be viewed by human eyes. But many electrocardiogram ([11](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r11)) and Papanicolaou test ([12](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r12)) interpretations have been human-free for years. Recent research predicts that workflows combining human and AI expertise can forgo human review of 63% of screening mammograms while increasing overall accuracy ([13](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r13)). Because screening is only a small part of radiologist work, these systems may slow the growth of the radiologist workforce but will not displace radiologists.

5. LLMs Will Transform Patients’ Understanding of Radiology
-----------------------------------------------------------

Over the past year, LLMs have captivated the medical world, explaining medical guidelines ([14](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r14)), drafting radiology reports ([8](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r8)), and even passing board examinations ([15](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r15)). But LLMs have trouble with simple math ([16](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r16)), make potentially harmful errors in differential diagnosis ([17](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r17)), and sometimes authoritatively spout falsehoods ([8](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r8)). As we learn more about the capabilities of LLMs, we will likely find more problems that inhibit the regulatory clearance of these incredibly powerful tools for medical diagnosis ([18](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r18)).

But LLMs will soon be deployed for other radiology applications. Regulations against information blocking give patients ready access to their medical information ([19](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r19)). But the terminology radiologists use to communicate with requesting clinicians can mystify patients. The ability of LLMs to summarize information at an arbitrary reading level in the patient’s preferred language will help patients understand their reports ([20](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r20)). For example, here is the response of ChatGPT, an LLM developed by OpenAI, when asked to explain the circle of Willis at a fifth-grade reading level ([21](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r21)):

“The circle of Willis is a group of blood vessels in your brain that helps to provide blood to your brain and keep it healthy. It is shaped like a circle, and it is made up of several large blood vessels that are connected to each other. These blood vessels are called arteries, and they carry blood from your heart to your brain. The circle of Willis helps to make sure that your brain gets enough blood, even if one of the arteries becomes blocked or narrowed.”

These patient-friendly explanations of radiology reports will transform patients’ relationship with their imaging information and will bring radiologists and patients closer together.

6. Multimodal AI Will Discover New Uses for Diagnostic Images
-------------------------------------------------------------

The human visual perception system has evolved over millions of years to discern patterns; thus, AI models only rarely find patterns humans haven’t yet recognized. As a result, the best AI models exhibit performance comparable to expert humans on most visual tasks.

As the mountain of digital health data grows, massive multimodal data sets will link imaging studies to troves of data from genomics, clinical notes, laboratory values, and wearable devices ([22](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r22)). Self-supervised learning methods ([23](https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/radiol.231114#r23)), which do not require expensive data labeling, will produce “generalist” models that encode relationships among many different data types. These models will uncover new associations beyond the capabilities of human information processing, showing how imaging appearances relate to specific genomic signatures and laboratory values.

Insights from multimodal data sets will change how we stage cancer and other complex diseases. For example, the TNM staging of cancer is scaled to fit the human memory system, relying on a small number of categories representing cancer size, location, and spread. Multimodal AI models will precisely quantify disease burden and produce more accurate predictions of disease course.

The Future of Radiology And Artificial Intelligence
===================================================

Radiologists’ creative work will be necessary in the future to solve complex issues and supervising diagnostic processes; but AI will definitely become part of their daily routine in diagnosing simpler cases and taking over repetitive tasks.

![Image 7: Dr. Bertalan Mesko, PhD](https://medicalfuturist.com/images/dr-bertalan-mesko-2022.jpg)

##### **Dr. Bertalan Mesko, PhD**

13 min |  14 June 2024

![Image 8: Future of Radiology](https://cdn.medicalfuturist.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/future-of-radiology-1-768x470.jpg%20300w)

Key Takeaways
-------------

What if an algorithm could tell you whether you have cancer based on your CT scan or mammography exam?

* * *

Radiologists’ creative work will be necessary in the future to solve complex issues and supervising diagnostic processes; but AI will definitely become part of their daily routine in diagnosing simpler cases and taking over repetitive tasks.

* * *

Rather than getting threatened by it, we should familiarize with how it could help change the course of radiology for the better.

* * *

What if an algorithm could tell you whether you have cancer based on your CT scan or mammography exam? While I am confident that radiologists’ creative work will be necessary in the future to solve complex issues and supervise diagnostic processes, A.I. will definitely become part of their daily routine in diagnosing simpler cases and taking over repetitive tasks. So rather than getting threatened by it, we should familiarise ourselves with how it could help change the course of radiology for the better.

**Radiologists who use AI will replace those who don’t**
--------------------------------------------------------

There is a lot of hype and plenty of fear around artificial intelligence and its impact on the future of healthcare. There are many signs pointing toward the fact that [A.I. will completely move the world of medicine](https://medicalfuturist.com/artificial-intelligence-will-redesign-healthcare/). As deep learning algorithms and narrow A.I. started to buzz especially around the field of medical imaging, many radiologists went into panic mode.

![Image 9](https://cdn.medicalfuturist.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/AI-not-replace-physicians-768x768.jpg)
Bradley Erickson, Director of the Radiology Informatics Lab at Mayo Clinic told me that some of the hype we hear from some of the machine learning and deep learning experts saying that A.I. would replace radiologists is looking at radiologists as if they were just looking at pictures. _That would be me saying while I look at programmers, all they do is typing, so we can replace a programmer with a speech recognition system_, he added. Langlotz compared the situation to that of the autopilot in aviation. The innovation did not replace real pilots, it only augmented their tasks. On very long flights, it is handy to turn on the autopilot, but they are useless when you need rapid judgment. So, the combination of humans and machines is the winning solution. And it will be the same in healthcare.

Thus, I agree with Langlotz completely when he says that _artificial intelligence will not replace radiologists. Yet, those radiologists who use A.I. will replace the ones who don’t_. Let me show you why.

**What do cat intestines, X-ray lamps and the history of medical imaging have in common?**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The field of clinical radiology started obviously with the quite [coincidental discovery of the X-ray by Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen on 8 November 1895 in Würzburg, Germany](http://static.springer.com/sgw/documents/1426506/application/pdf/Van+Gelderen_A+Brief+History+of+Radiology.pdf). Within two months, the X-ray mania ran over the world. Sensational headlines in newspapers propagated the “new light seeing through flesh to bones”, while one inventor even speculated that “soon every house will have a cathode-ray machine”. Any similarities about hyped technologies coming to mind?

![Image 10: Future of Radiology](https://1712507217.rsc.cdn77.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/wilhelm-conrad-r%C3%B6ntgen-and-x-ray.png)
Thomas Edison became so excited about the new discovery that he even wanted to create a commercial “X-ray lamp” (unfortunately, his efforts failed) and tried to get an X-ray of the human brain in action (sadly, that was not a success either). His latter endeavour let story-driven reporters go nuts: they were allegedly waiting for the innovation outside his laboratory for weeks in vain. Some went as far as to fabricate images about the human brain. One of them turned out to be a pan of cat intestines radiographed in 1896 by H. A. Falk!

While some early efforts turned out to be huge blows and impossible projects, X-rays got acclimatised in medicine. Something similar will happen with A.I. and healthcare soon. I hope with fewer cat intestines, though.

**Radiology has been the playfield of technological development since the beginnings**
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In the TV series, _The Knick_ depicting the first decades of modern surgery and healthcare, an inventor gets in touch with the hospital manager in his office to present him with a new idea, the X-ray machine. It turns out that it takes an hour or so for the brand-new machine to take the picture! Currently, if you go to the hospital to get the annual check-up on your lungs done, the X-ray procedure will take a couple of minutes in a fortunate situation, and some more until you get the results.

Please accept cookies to access this content

Plenty has changed since those experiments with the ‘X-ray lamp’, but one thing was constant: rapid technological development in radiology.

**A bigger range of tools and higher precision**
------------------------------------------------

Approximately half a century after the discovery of the X-ray, ultrasound joined the methods of medical imaging. From the mid-sixties onwards, the advent of commercially available systems allowed wider dissemination. Rapid technological advances in electronics and piezoelectric materials provided further improvements from bistable to greyscale images and from still images to real-time moving images. And it is also amazing to see how we went from room-sized, clumsy ultrasound machines to portable ones circa within another half of a century! In 2016, Clarius Mobile Health introduced the world’s first handheld ultrasound scanner with a mobile application. The doctor can carry around the personal ultrasound device for quick exams and to guide procedures such as nerve blocks and targeted injections.

Now, let’s look at body scanners. The first CT scanners were[introduced in 1971 with a single detector for brain study under the leadership of Godfrey Hounsfield](https://radiopaedia.org/articles/ct-scanner-evolution), an electrical engineer at EMI (Electric and Musical Industries, Ltd). The very first [MRI scanner was built by Raymond Damadian in the 1970s by hand](http://www.two-views.com/mri-imaging/history.html#sthash.RbPBZETW.dpbs), assisted by his students at New York’s Downstate Medical Center. He achieved the first MRI scan of a healthy human body in 1977 and a human organism with cancer in 1978. The first functional MR imaging of the human brain is produced in the early 1990s. By the early 2000s, cardiac MRI, body MRI, fetal imaging, and functional MR imaging became routine exams in many imaging centers.

![Image 11: Future of Radiology](https://1712507217.rsc.cdn77.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/medical-imaging-techniques.jpg)
**Along with precision comes automation**

Thus, the history of radiology shows the expansion of means as well as the increase in precision so far. While the latter is still in focus, there is also a visible shift towards making radiologists’ lives easier by automation. As radiologists need to go through more and more images every day, it becomes inevitable that part of their job can be automated. When we can train algorithms to spot and detect many types of abnormalities based on radiology images, why wouldn’t we let it do the time-consuming job so we can let radiologists dedicate their precious focus to the hardest issues?

When deep learning becomes possible and the algorithm could teach itself while radiologists rate its effectiveness, it’s going to get better just by working more. This is an opportunity we have to grab. This way radiology would be one of the most creative specialities in which problem-solving and a holistic approach would be the key.

So, it certainly would not mean that A.I. would take over all the tasks of radiologists. As Erickson put it, if you look at the frequency of findings and diagnoses on medical images _, there are the common ones where AI_ _could help, but there is a really long tail, uncommon but really important things that we cannot miss_. He believes that it is going to be difficult for deep learning algorithms to identify those. But where do we stand with technology at the moment?

**Could AI****predict whether you would die soon?**
---------------------------------------------------

Scientists at the University of Adelaide have been experimenting with an [AI system that is said to be able to tell if you are going to die](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-017-01931-w#Fig4). By analysing CT scans from 48 patients, the deep learning algorithms could predict whether they’d die within five years with 69 percent accuracy. It is “broadly similar” to scores from human diagnosticians, the paper says. It is an impressive achievement. The deep learning system was trained to analyse over 16,000 image features that could indicate signs of disease in those organs. Researchers say that their goal is for the algorithm to measure overall health rather than spot a single disease.

![Image 12: FDA-approved AI-based algorithms](https://cdn.medicalfuturist.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/fda-approvals-ai-algorithms-768x660.png)

To view the infographics in full size, right click and open in a new browser tab/window

This is just one of the numerous initiatives on developing artificial intelligence applications to support the field of radiology. You can take a look at this article published by The Medical Futurist Institute in [npj Digital Medicine journal](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-020-00324-0), or [the online database](https://medicalfuturist.com/fda-approved-ai-based-algorithms/) we keep updating ever since. It currently has 79 entries, of which 39 belong to the field of radiology. That is undisputedly the medical field with the highest number of AI initiatives.

However, the ongoing research does not mean that we are already at the stage where average patients will have to face their exact life expectancy based on their medical images when they go to the hospital.

**What are the challenges in introducing AI****to the radiology department?**
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

In order to have some estimation of when machine learning might be introduced on a wider scale, we have to look at how machine learning takes place in radiology. The process usually goes like this: the algorithm should be fed by thousands, if not millions of images and learn to spot differences regarding tissues. Just as in the case of computers recognising images of dogs and cats. If the algorithm makes a mistake, the researcher notices it and adjusts the code. Thus, it is a rather lengthy process that needs tons of available data. Erickson believes that the result will look like the following: we’ll do the high volume exam, and the algorithm will probably create a structured, minable, preliminary report. _So it will do the quantification that most humans hate to do and it will do that very well_, he noted.

Anna Fernandez, Health Informatics/Precision Medicine Lead at Booz Allen Hamilton told me though that _there are several challenges in building these discovery and analytic platforms – from acquiring access and ingesting the data, sufficiently annotating the data, storage strategy, governance/policy use throughout, and types of analysis enabled via the platform._ The biggest challenge is sufficiently annotating the data to allow different views of it (full right to owners, restricted subset to others) and enable discovery across the connected data sets in the platform.

![Image 13: Future of Radiology](https://1712507217.rsc.cdn77.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/ai-and-radiology.jpg)
Moreover, hospitals also need to be convinced that A.I. algorithms work. Fernandez believes that it will be a step-wise process by for example taking advantage of hybrid internal and external “crowdsourcing” with sufficiently anonymised data.

_For example, a vendor can have established data science algorithms based on anonymised data from their hospital network, then the new hospital can employ the algorithm and further refine it to the anonymised “local” data sets (that may include additional patient variables) to customise it to their population._ As the hospitals see a “win,” they may be encouraged to release a more restricted anonymised data set to contribute back to the vendor solution. So it’s a little bit similar to how you try to go into the cold water on a hot summer day. First, you look at other people doing it, then you realise it’s safe, so you put your toes in the water before entirely going under.

**We get to have AI analyzing our CT scans**
--------------------------------------------

How can we depict in a concise and easy-to-grasp way how the human-A.I. collaboration will unfold in the field of medicine in the years and decades to come? Andrew Ng, founder of[deeplearning.ai](https://www.deeplearning.ai/) described five levels of automation. The Medical Futurist implemented this concept in medicine, and explained these levels with current examples and future scenarios [in this article](https://medicalfuturist.com/5-levels-of-automation-in-medicine/).

Below is such an infographic that helps in visualising the spectrum of automation in medicine, ranging from human-only (level1) to fully automated (level5).

![Image 14](https://cdn.medicalfuturist.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/tmf-5-levels-of-automation-768x1292.png)
Future radiology is expected to work with level 3 (AI assistance) and level 4 (Partial automation) algorithms. What do these mean?

**At the third level**, the AI system supports physicians in clinical decision-making via suggestions. For example, after scanning a database of chest CT scans, the A.I. considers the chest CT results of a patient being investigated and highlights suspicious signs. These signs are then further investigated by the physician.

**On level four**, with partial automation, an AI system can come up with its own diagnosis; but if it’s not confident enough about it, the AI turns to physicians for help. Several companies are working on such solutions today. A [practical example](https://beholdai.wordpress.com/2022/08/10/southend_backlog/) is Behold’s Class IIa CE marked platform that is used by UK’s NHS Trust to help clear the radiology backlogs in lung cancer screening. It can process adult frontal Chest X-Ray examinations and has two key outputs. It either flags the image as “suspected lung cancer” and prioritises the patient for a radiologist consultation, or it identifies the image as normal – although the image will also be audited by a radiologist.

Palo Alto-based Nines[developed an AI-system](https://medcitynews.com/2020/04/nines-gets-fda-clearance-for-ai-to-flag-two-life-threatening-conditions/?rf=1)that can identify potential cases of intracranial haemorrhage and mass effect from CT scans. It then flags those cases for radiologists to review.

**Radiology’s Future is AI**
----------------------------

All in all, research trends and experts underline how AI will revolutionise radiology in the long term. Thus, rather than neglecting it or feeling threatened by it, the medical community should embrace its achievements.

Abstract
--------

The transformative power of artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping radiology, medicine, and healthcare, marking radiology as a pioneering specialty in AI adoption. The digital nature of radiological data and standardized data formats positioned radiology as the ideal testing ground for clinical AI integration. While initial enthusiasm led to inflated expectations, fueled by linear thinking and the planning fallacy, AI has now matured into tools that augment, rather than replace, radiologists’ expertise. Radiologists’ role is evolving from image interpreters to diagnostic orchestrators in a multimodal era. The integration of imaging data with diverse sources such as genomics, pathology, and wearable sensors necessitates a shift to a systems-level perspective. This transformation demands not only technical literacy but also interdisciplinary collaboration to effectively synthesize AI-driven insights and mitigate cognitive overload. Radiologists must navigate uncertainty, adopt structured workflows, and communicate AI-supported findings clearly to maintain trust in diagnostics. The emergence of generative AI, particularly large language models, further streamlines AI adoption by enabling intuitive, human-centered interfaces. However, addressing the growing knowledge gap is crucial. Traditional radiology training must be overhauled to incorporate data science, bioinformatics, and systems biology, ensuring radiologists are prepared to lead multimodal diagnostics. Radiologists are uniquely positioned to spearhead this transition, leveraging AI to integrate diverse data streams, improve patient care, and foster collaboration across specialties. Proactive adaptation will secure radiologists’ central role in AI-driven medicine, safeguarding the human element in healthcare while advancing diagnostic precision.

Introduction
------------

> “Twenty-five years ago, the World Wide Web was just getting off the ground. Therefore, when thinking of the medical research landscape in 25 years, it is reasonable to think big and without limits.” – Eric Topol [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib1).

This quote from Eric Topol captures the essence of the transformative period we find ourselves in today. The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping the field of radiology, medicine and society as a whole. In medicine, Radiology, as a digital-first specialty, has become a testing ground for AI applications in medicine and is positioned as a leader in the adoption of AI-driven technologies. The question we now face is not whether AI will play a role in the future of radiology, but how radiologists will adapt and lead this change and whether they are even willing to seize this historic opportunity to reshape healthcare for the better.

The early adoption of AI in radiology
-------------------------------------

Radiology was one of the first medical specialties to embrace AI, and this was no coincidence. The digital nature of radiological data, standardized formats such as DICOM [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib2), and the early adoption of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) following the “ImageNet moment” [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib3) provided a fertile ground for AI integration. CNNs, designed for image processing, were naturally suited to radiological applications, making radiology an ideal field for initial AI deployment. However, the initial excitement surrounding AI’s potential quickly led to inflated expectations. Predictions that AI would soon replace radiologists, including a high-profile statement [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib4) from pioneer and now Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, reflected a common cognitive bias: the tendency to think linearly in an exponential world.

The pitfalls of linear thinking in an exponential age
-----------------------------------------------------

Humans are prone to linear thinking, often underestimating the phases of exponential growth while overestimating short-term progress [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib5). This misjudgment is further aggravated by the _planning fallacy_, a concept introduced by Kahneman and Tversky [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib6). The planning fallacy describes our tendency to underestimate the time, costs, and risks associated with future tasks, even when we have historical data that suggests otherwise. In the context of AI development, many experts and stakeholders anticipated a rapid deployment of AI solutions in clinical practice, assuming a linear trajectory of progress based on early successes in research environments. This fallacy led to overly optimistic projections about the timeline for AI adoption and fueled unrealistic expectations about its capabilities, particularly in complex, real-world clinical scenarios.

This cognitive bias is evident in the adoption curve of many new technologies, as described by the Gartner Hype Cycle [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib7). In the case of AI in radiology, the initial wave of enthusiasm gave way to disillusionment as it became clear that early AI models, while promising, were not ready to fully replace human expertise.

Radiology has now moved beyond this initial hype phase. We are witnessing a steady, incremental integration of AI tools that assist rather than replace radiologists, focusing on enhancing productivity and reducing cognitive load [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib8). AI is being used to automate routine, mundane tasks, allowing radiologists to concentrate on more complex, intellectually demanding cases. However, recognizing the influence of the planning fallacy on our past assumptions should encourage a more tempered and realistic approach as we continue to integrate AI into medical practice. By acknowledging the challenges of scaling technology in clinical settings, we can better manage expectations and develop strategies that align with the gradual, iterative nature of true innovation.

Radiologists as orchestrators in a multimodal era
-------------------------------------------------

As Jha and Topol noted in their seminal JAMA article [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib9), the role of the radiologist is evolving from a traditional image interpreter to an information specialist. This shift is not merely a cosmetic change but a fundamental transformation driven by the increasing complexity and volume of diagnostic data. Radiologists have always relied on additional clinical information - such as laboratory tests, clinical notes, and pathology results - because interpreting imaging findings in the context of the overall clinical picture leads to more accurate and meaningful diagnoses.

The same principle applies to AI models. There is a growing trend toward multimodal AI systems that integrate imaging data with other non-imaging inputs. These multimodal approaches have the potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy and clinical utility, mirroring how radiologists synthesize information today. By combining diverse data sources, such models are expected to dominate the field in the future, driving a new era of precision diagnostics and improved patient care.

The growing availability of data from sources such as molecular diagnostics, genomic profiles, electronic health records, and wearable sensors - while undeniably making multimodal AI more powerful - also introduces new challenges for radiologists. This marks a fundamental shift in how radiologists approach diagnostics. Traditionally, diagnosis has been a largely linear process centered around the interpretation of a single data stream, primarily imaging, complemented by basic clinical information. In the multimodal era, however, this process is becoming increasingly non-linear, requiring the simultaneous synthesis of multiple, often disparate, data sources.

To navigate this complexity, radiologists must adopt a more holistic, systems-level perspective. Their role is evolving into that of a diagnostic orchestrator, coordinating, validating or rejecting, weighing, and synthesizing AI-generated insights drawn from a vast and varied array of information streams. This transformation not only demands a shift in mindset but also new skills to ensure that multimodal AI serves as an enabler of better patient care rather than an additional layer of complexity.

The success of this transformation relies on the establishment of efficient human-machine teaming, as emphasized by Henry et al. [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib10). Rather than replacing clinical judgment, AI systems must be designed to complement and enhance diagnostic and treatment management processes. Acting as collaborative partners, these tools serve as a _second pair of eyes_, assisting radiologists in identifying potential issues and alleviating cognitive burden. This allows radiologists to focus on the most critical and complex aspects of patient care. By fostering this synergistic relationship, AI not only enhances decision-making but also preserves and amplifies radiologists’ expertise, driving a more efficient and accurate diagnostic process.

The advent of generative AI, particularly large language models (LLMs), represents a paradigm shift in human-machine interaction [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib11). Traditionally, healthcare providers had to adapt to the rigid, prescriptive nature of machines, tailoring their inputs to accommodate technological constraints. With LLMs, this dynamic is fundamentally reversed: machines now adapt to human communication, understanding natural language and responding intuitively in a conversational manner. This revolution in interaction simplifies the integration of AI into daily clinical workflows, enabling radiologists to seamlessly access, interpret, and act on complex, multimodal insights. Ultimately, this enhances their capacity to deliver more accurate and high-quality patient care.

However, this shift also introduces significant cognitive and practical challenges. The human brain is inherently limited in its ability to process vast and varied streams of information simultaneously [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib14). While experienced radiologists excel at contextualizing imaging findings through years of clinical expertise, the integration of increasingly complex, non-visual data—such as whole-genome sequencing results or proteomic analyses—risks overwhelming their cognitive capacity. If not carefully managed, we may merely replace information overload on a lower data level with a new kind of overload at the results and interpretation level.

To address this, workflows must be thoughtfully designed to align with the human brain’s inherent limitations in parallel processing. AI tools and systems should be tailored to filter, prioritize, and present information in a way that enhances human strengths - such as moral reasoning, ethical judgment, oversight, self-correction, and adaptability - rather than competing with them. By supporting radiologists as decision-makers rather than inundating them with unmanageable insights, we can ensure that AI augments clinical expertise without compromising efficiency or accuracy. This approach will foster a more balanced, effective, and sustainable integration of multimodal AI into diagnostic workflows.

Furthermore, the evolving role of radiologists as information specialists brings up an important issue of legitimacy and trust. As AI systems become more sophisticated and their recommendations more opaque, there is a growing need for radiologists to act as intermediaries between AI outputs and clinical decisions. This role requires not only technical expertise but also strong communication skills to effectively convey complex, data-driven insights to clinicians and patients. Without this interpretative layer, there is a risk that the diagnostic process could become a _black box_, undermining the trust that patients place in the medical system and clinicians place in radiologists [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib16). Radiologists must therefore rise to the challenge, embracing their expanded role as the linchpins of a data-rich, AI-enhanced diagnostic landscape.

However, fully embracing the role of an information specialist requires radiologists also to strike a delicate balance between acknowledging uncertainty and providing decisive, evidence-based guidance. Radiologists have traditionally managed diagnostic uncertainty by offering broad differential diagnoses, adopting a purely descriptive approach in their reports or referring to the limitations of image quality or the sufficiency of available data. This cautious approach stems from the weight of their responsibility; a single misinterpretation can lead to serious consequences, potentially impacting a patient’s treatment and even determining life or death outcomes. As radiologists take on a broader role in the diagnostic process, integrating diverse data sources and guiding clinical decisions with multimodal AI, the magnitude of their responsibility increases, amplifying the inherent uncertainty. This amplified uncertainty can potentially lead to diagnostic hesitancy, where radiologists may become even more cautious in their interpretations or rely excessively on caveats and qualifications. Such an approach, while understandable given the stakes, may inadvertently reduce the clarity and actionable value of their diagnostic reports. To address this issue, it is essential to foster a culture of shared responsibility and collaborative decision-making in clinical practice. By integrating multidisciplinary teams and leveraging the collective expertise of specialists from various fields, radiologists can mitigate the burden of individual uncertainty. Regular case discussions, interdisciplinary consultations, and the use of boards or diagnostic conferences can help validate complex interpretations, providing a safety net for radiologists as they navigate the complexities of multimodal data.

Bridging the knowledge gap: from image interpreters to multimodal information specialists
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The rise of multimodal AI in radiology will mark a significant shift in the diagnostic landscape, driven by the superior performance of these models compared to traditional unimodal approaches [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib17). These sophisticated AI systems can integrate diverse data inputs, ranging from imaging and lab results to complex genomic profiles and histopathological analyses. However, the effective supervision and utilization of such multimodal tools demand a profound understanding not necessarily of the AI algorithms themselves but of the diverse types of medical data they process.

This transformation raises critical questions about the qualifications and preparedness of radiologists for this new role. Traditional radiology training focuses heavily on mastering the nuances of image interpretation and understanding the physics behind various imaging modalities, such as MRI and CT. While these skills remain essential, they are no longer sufficient in an era where diagnostic decisions must incorporate inputs from a broad spectrum of medical data, including complex non-imaging information such as genomic sequences, proteomic analyses, and advanced immunological markers. The challenge is that these data types often fall outside the typical expertise of a radiologist, creating a significant knowledge gap.

This gap is exacerbated by the rapid development of multimodal AI models that are capable of processing and analyzing not only images but also text, lab results, and time-series data from wearable biosensors or electrocardiograms. These models have the potential to provide unprecedented insights, but they also introduce new layers of complexity. For example, a multimodal AI system might integrate imaging findings with genomic data to suggest a targeted therapy or flag a potential genetic predisposition. However, without a solid understanding of the underlying genomics or the context of these findings, radiologists may struggle to interpret the results effectively or assess the validity of the AI’s recommendations.

The current educational framework for radiologists does not adequately prepare them for this evolving landscape. Although radiologists are trained in general medicine during their years at medical school, the focus of their specialty training narrows significantly to image analysis and interpretation. The rapid pace of advancements in fields like genomics, molecular pathology or systems biology has far outstripped the traditional medical curriculum, leading to a situation where many practicing radiologists may not have the up-to-date knowledge necessary to fully leverage or critically evaluate these new multimodal AI tools. Moreover, the half-life of medical knowledge is shrinking, meaning that what was once cutting-edge information during medical school may now be outdated or even obsolete. This presents a significant challenge for radiologists who must continuously update their skills and knowledge to keep pace, not even mentioning innovations in AI and data science. As a result, there is a pressing need to reevaluate and expand the educational framework for radiologists, equipping them with both the interdisciplinary medical expertise and the technical literacy required to effectively oversee and integrate multimodal AI into clinical practice.

The traditional curriculum, focused primarily on image interpretation and modality-specific expertise, is no longer sufficient in an era of multimodal AI. A comprehensive overhaul is needed - one that integrates foundational courses in data science, bioinformatics, and systems biology with core radiology training. This expanded curriculum should equip radiologists with the technical literacy to understand the principles behind AI algorithms, critically assess their outputs, and incorporate these insights seamlessly into clinical decision-making.

A key element of this transformation is the inclusion of interdisciplinary training. Radiologists must go beyond their traditional focus and develop expertise that intersects with genomics, pathology, oncology, laboratory medicine, immunology, molecular biology, computational medicine, and many more. By collaborating closely with specialists in these fields, radiologists can gain a deeper understanding of the diverse data inputs that modern AI systems analyze, enabling them to effectively interpret complex, multimodal outputs. This shift towards an interdisciplinary approach is essential to bridge the current knowledge gap and to ensure that radiologists are not merely passive users of AI but active participants in the development and refinement of these tools.

Additionally, radiology training programs must adapt to prepare future radiologists for this expanded role. Emphasis should be placed not only on medical knowledge, technical skills, and AI literacy but also on building confidence in decision-making under uncertainty. Drawing from Tversky and Kahneman’s research [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib5), we recognize that radiologists - as all humans - are prone to cognitive biases, such as overconfidence in heuristic-based judgments or the _illusion of validity_, where they may rely excessively on pattern recognition without fully accounting for statistical uncertainties. Therefore, training should include risk assessment and probabilistic reasoning to counteract these biases and improve the accuracy of clinical judgments. Developing skills in effective communication of probabilistic findings and structured reporting can help radiologists articulate their conclusions clearly, even when faced with ambiguous or conflicting data [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib19).

However, realizing this vision requires more than an update to the curriculum - it necessitates a significant cultural shift within the field of radiology. Proactive planning and strategic foresight are needed to redefine the role of radiologists as _integrative information specialists_. This involves embracing continuous learning and fostering a mindset that values interdisciplinary collaboration. By embedding these principles into the educational framework, we can prepare radiologists to navigate the increasing complexity of multimodal data and collaborate effectively with bioinformaticians, geneticists, and other specialists - or ultimately merge various diagnostic disciplines into a new field of interdisciplinary clinical information specialists, maybe then referred to as _superdiagnosticians_.

Moreover, there is a pressing need to formalize the role of the radiologist as a leader in diagnostic integration. Radiologists are uniquely positioned to bridge the gap between diverse diagnostic domains, given their central role in patient care and their experience in synthesizing imaging data with clinical context. By embracing this expanded role, radiologists can leverage AI tools to integrate imaging findings with laboratory results, genomic data, and pathology reports, providing a holistic view of the patient’s condition. This shift towards a more integrative approach will not only enhance the diagnostic process but also solidify the role of radiology at the forefront of patient care.

The strategic role of radiology in leading AI adoption
------------------------------------------------------

Radiology is uniquely positioned to lead the charge towards _Superdiagnostics_, an era where diagnostic capabilities are enhanced by sophisticated, multimodal AI systems. Radiologists are already accustomed to synthesizing information from various sources and collaborating across specialties, making them ideal candidates to oversee the integration of these advanced tools. In fact, the groundwork for this leadership role has been laid over the past several years, as radiology was the first medical specialty to witness the clinical implementation of AI tools. Currently, the majority of regulatory approved AI applications in medicine are found in radiology, underscoring its role as the digital pioneer among medical disciplines.

Future trends in AI-powered radiology
=====================================

![Image 9: Kees Wesdorp](https://deephealth.com/app/uploads/2025/01/kees-pp-150x150.jpeg)

Kees Wesdorp

President and CEO DeepHealth

May 28, 2025

5 min

![Image 10](https://deephealth.com/app/uploads/2025/03/thumbnail_DEEPHEALTH_Thumbnaill_5-future-trends-in-AI-powered-radiology_R4-NoLines.jpg)

The prevalence of artificial intelligence (AI) in healthcare – particularly in radiology – has increased sharply in recent years. According to the US Food & Drug administration, more than 1,000 AI-enabled medical devices have been authorized to be sold in the US – and 76% of those are in radiology.[[1]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn1)

AI is rapidly reshaping the radiology landscape, and this is especially evident in cancer detection. AI-powered breast screening, for example, has shown to increase cancer detection rates by an impressive 21%.[[2]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn2)In prostate cancer, AI assistance has the potential to shrink the number of missed prostate findings to only 1% in cases where radiologists alone would have missed 8% of clinically significant cancer instances.[[3]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn3) We are only beginning to harness AI’s transformative potential, not only to improve disease detection but also to reduce unnecessary imaging and ensure high-quality radiology care is available and accessible for more patients.

Here are five AI trends in radiology that we expect to see more of in the not-too-distant future.

Streamline workflow and connect care teams
------------------------------------------

The modern radiology workflow is too often siloed – different people using standalone AI tools in separate systems, managing disconnected data. Fully benefiting from AI in clinical practice requires end-to-end integration that gives everyone in the radiology care pathway timely access to the information and capabilities they need. Envision a cloud-native operating system at the center of radiology operations that would unify data and connect the clinical and operational workflow. The end-to-end AI-powered informatics layer brings AI to help when needed, such as with workflow automation or clinical assistance.

The modern operations suite would replace a typical Radiology Information System (RIS) and would deliver advanced capabilities via personalized AI-powered workspaces for well-coordinated health actions across the workspaces. From patient onboarding through image acquisition, reporting and follow-up, this comprehensive approach would make sure the right information was available to the right people at the right time, streamlining operations and enabling a more seamless experience for care teams and patients.

[Discover DeepHealth’s Operations Suite™](https://deephealth.com/enterprise-imaging/operations-suite/)

Advanced visualization and diagnostic capabilities
--------------------------------------------------

On the clinical side, integrating an end-to-end informatics layer with AI-powered diagnostic solutions sets the stage for advanced medical imaging interpretation. More powerful than the traditional Picture Archiving System (PACS), a cloud-native ‘diagnostic suite’ approach would bring functionalities together in a unified diagnostic workspace and enable practices to leverage AI-powered features like advanced 3-D image visualization, a real-time viewer capable of sub-second image streaming, a smart worklist to prioritize the right cases, AI-enhanced reporting to help radiologists report faster, workflow analytics for better decision making, remote collaboration, advanced image and data management, and built-in AI orchestration – all accessed through a single, cohesive interface. These powerful new visualization and diagnostic capabilities would better position medical imaging practices to address crucial market demands for enterprise imaging.

[Discover DeepHealth’s Diagnostic Suite™](https://deephealth.com/enterprise-imaging/diagnostic-suite/)

Unleash the full power of screening
-----------------------------------

While population screening efforts make earlier stage cancer detection possible for more patients, the workload these high-volume programs create for radiologists is a significant barrier. Even for experienced radiologists, reading and interpreting scans is complex and time-consuming. But combining AI-powered workflow efficiencies with AI’s clinical gains – like bringing generalist radiologists up to expert levels,[[4]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn4) reducing unnecessary recalls,[[5]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn5) and cutting down reporting time[[6]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn6) – makes imaging for high volume screening more feasible and scalable.

Already in the US, AI powers the Enhanced Breast Cancer Detection program, a mammography screening initiative that processes roughly 1.4 million scans each year. Radiologists are equipped with AI-powered tools to quickly and accurately detect even subtle lesions. In Europe, the NHS England’s Targeted Lung Check Program uses an AI-powered lung solution[[7]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn7) to assist radiologists in one of the world’s largest lung cancer screening efforts, where data from the UK government shows 76% of cancers detected were found at earlier stages.[[8]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn8) These programs have the potential to not only improve outcomes but also help address health disparities in the midst of radiologist shortages. Expect to see more and broader disease screening and imaging-based population health initiatives made possible with the help of AI.

[Discover DeepHealth’s Population Health solutions](https://deephealth.com/population-health/)

Smart technologies for major care advances
------------------------------------------

Up-and-coming ‘smart technologies’ that bring AI-powered informatics closer to modalities – like mammography, for example – would accelerate AI adoption and enable a more seamless care delivery experience. These advancements in imaging could also usher in exciting new possibilities for medical advancement. According to DeepHealth Chief Operating and Technology Officer Sham Sokka, integrating DeepHealth’s breast AI into mammography systems could make a complete AI breast readout possible in less than five minutes, relieving the stress of waiting for results. Patients with diagnostic findings would know right away and could begin their diagnosis and treatment journey, while those without could go home directly after the scan with peace of mind that they are cancer-free. Smart technologies that deeply embed AI-powered informatics with medical devices could make breakthroughs possible in many areas – autonomous x-ray, for example.

[Discover DeepHealth’s SmartTechnology™](https://deephealth.com/insights/hear-from-sham-sokka-on-the-future-of-smarttechnology/)

Remote capability to increase capacity and site coordination
------------------------------------------------------------

Radiology teams are stretched thin meeting increasing demands for faster, more precise scans, while managing higher volumes and coordinating across distributed teams. AI-powered remote platforms can help tackle these challenges head-on by enabling teams to remotely acquire images and seamlessly collaborate and allocate resources across multiple locations. Radiologists can review and interpret imaging studies from anywhere, reducing bottlenecks at high-volume locations. Specialized radiologists don’t need to be on site to provide second opinions or handle complex cases. Technologists at different sites can receive real-time assistance from experts, ensuring consistent imaging quality across all locations. Patients benefit from being able to access high-quality radiology care, no matter their location, and practices can more efficiently scale resources, optimize workflows and improve efficiency. Remote capability ensures access to the right expertise, when and where needed and AI can be used to scale remote capabilities enabling remote experts to support more sites with less effort.

[Discover DeepHealth’s TechLive™ collaboration platform](https://deephealth.com/enterprise-imaging/techlive/)

No doubt, AI will continue to have a transformative impact on healthcare. Developing solutions that perform effectively across diverse populations requires utilizing large, comprehensive data sets to build and validate the algorithms. AI companies will need to train and test on a wide variety of patient data to ensure their solutions perform well across diverse patient subgroups. [[9]](https://deephealth.com/insights/future-trends-in-ai-powered-radiology/#_ftn9) This will help ensure that we fully harness AI’s great potential to solve clinical challenges, expand access to high-quality radiology care, and enable better health outcomes.

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI will increasingly assist radiologists by automating routine tasks, enhancing diagnostic accuracy, and integrating multimodal data for more informed decision-making.
Future radiology will see AI seamlessly integrated with cloud computing, enabling advanced functionalities like real-time collaboration, automated reporting, and comprehensive imaging workflows.

AI's integration will reshape healthcare delivery, improving patient outcomes through faster diagnostics, reducing costs, and enabling personalized treatment plans based on comprehensive data analysis.
